conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can provide you with accurate guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To help determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who's 8 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with you.

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great. Now, are you the person responsible for Alex? In other words, do you provide for Alex's needs such as food, clothes, and general care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm responsible for Alex and provide for all their needs - food, clothes, and everything else they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. One final question: Is Alex currently in hospital or care for an extended period, or do they live with someone else for any reason?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care for a couple of months, but they come home to stay with me regularly - they spend quite a bit of time at home each week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. This is an important detail for determining your eligibility.

Can you tell me more specifically how much time Alex spends at home with you each week? For example, do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home, or perhaps spend at least 2 nights in a row each week with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they do spend at least 24 hours a week at home with me. They come home on the weekends and stay with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:
- Alex is 8 years old (under 16)
- You live in the UK
- You are responsible for Alex and provide for all their needs
- Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Alex has been in care for a couple of months, but spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you

According to the official rules, while Child Benefit normally stops after 8 weeks when a child goes into care, there is an important exception: you can continue to receive Child Benefit if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you, even while they are in care.

Since Alex spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you, this exception applies to your situation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Even though Alex is in care, you qualify for Child Benefit because Alex spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you. This is a specific exception to the usual rule that Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks when a child goes into care.

You meet all the eligibility criteria:
- Alex is under 16 years old
- You live in the UK
- You are responsible for Alex and provide for their needs
- Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Alex spends sufficient time at home with you (at least 24 hours per week)

If you haven't already claimed, you should apply for Child Benefit. If you're already receiving it, you can continue to do so as long as these circumstances remain the same.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), which meets the age requirement. The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for Alex, providing for all their needs including food and clothes. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in care for a couple of months (which would normally mean Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks), there is an exception that applies: the claimant can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in care if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. Alex spends at least 24 hours a week at home with the claimant (coming home on weekends), so this exception applies. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old child Alex. Although Alex is in care, the claimant qualifies under the exception that allows Child Benefit to continue when a child in care spends at least 24 hours a week at home. All other standard eligibility criteria are met: the child is under 16, the claimant lives in the UK, is responsible for the child, and no one else is claiming for the same child.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), which meets the age requirement. The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for Alex, providing for all their needs including food and clothes. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in care for a couple of months (which would normally mean Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks), there is an exception that applies: the claimant can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in care if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. Alex spends at least 24 hours a week at home with the claimant (coming home on weekends), so this exception applies. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old child Alex. Although Alex is in care, the claimant qualifies under the exception that allows Child Benefit to continue when a child in care spends at least 24 hours a week at home. All other standard eligibility criteria are met: the child is under 16, the claimant lives in the UK, is responsible for the child, and no one else is claiming for the same child.
